section.title: The Baptism of Jesus
section.sectionRationale: Jesus steps out of obscurity and comes to the Jordan to be baptized by John. The sinless One submits to a baptism of repentance, insisting it fulfills all righteousness; and as He rises from the water heaven opens, the Spirit descends, and the Father speaks. In a few verses the whole Trinity is revealed and Jesus is publicly declared the beloved Son at the launch of His ministry.
section.thesis: By submitting to baptism the sinless Jesus identifies with the sinners He came to save and fulfills all righteousness, and the Father's voice and the Spirit's descent openly declare Him the beloved Son, anointing Him for the mission that now begins.
section.frameNote: Three beats: Jesus comes to be baptized and John objects; Jesus insists it is fitting to fulfill all righteousness; and heaven opens with the Spirit descending and the Father's voice declaring His Son.

units.label: John's objection
units.note: I need to be baptized by You

verses.text: At that time Jesus came from Galilee to the Jordan to be baptized by John.
But John tried to prevent Him, saying, “I need to be baptized by You, and do You come to me?”
“Let it be so now,” Jesus replied. “It is fitting for us to fulfill all {{dikaiosyne|righteousness}} in this way.” Then John permitted Him.

units.label: Heaven opened
units.note: the Spirit, and the Father's voice

verses.text: As soon as Jesus was baptized, He went up out of the water. Suddenly the heavens were opened, and He saw the {{pneuma|Spirit}} of God descending like a dove and resting on Him.
And a voice from heaven said, “This is My {{agapetos|beloved}} Son, in whom I am {{eudokeo|well pleased}}!”

dikaiosyne.gloss: Righteousness, conformity to God's will and covenant. Jesus is baptized to fulfill all righteousness, fully obeying the Father's plan and standing in the place of the people He came to save; the word looks ahead to the righteousness His whole life and death will secure.

pneuma.gloss: Spirit, breath, wind. The Spirit of God descends like a dove and rests on Jesus, anointing Him for His messianic ministry, the promised sign that He is the Servant on whom the Lord's Spirit remains, Isaiah 42:1 and 61:1.

agapetos.gloss: Beloved, dearly loved. The Father's word over Jesus, My beloved Son, echoes the royal Son of Psalm 2 and the chosen Servant of Isaiah 42, uniting kingship and sacrifice; the term was used of an only, cherished son.

eudokeo.gloss: To be well pleased, take delight in. The Father declares His full delight in the Son before a single miracle or sermon, grounding Jesus' whole ministry in the Father's settled love and approval.

basic.context: Jesus travels from Galilee to the Jordan and asks to be baptized by John. John protests, knowing that Jesus has no sins to confess and is far greater than he is. But Jesus insists it is fitting, to fulfill all righteousness. As He comes up from the water, three things happen at once: heaven opens, the Spirit descends like a dove, and the Father's voice declares Jesus His beloved Son.
basic.hermeneutics: This is the launch of Jesus' public ministry, and Matthew loads it with meaning. Read the baptism not as Jesus needing cleansing but as Jesus identifying with sinners and obeying the Father. The threefold revelation, Son in the water, Spirit descending, Father speaking, is one of the clearest windows in the Gospels onto the triune God.
basic.originalLanguages: Jesus is baptized to fulfill all righteousness, to do everything the Father's saving plan requires. The Spirit of God descends and rests on Him, anointing Him as the promised Servant. The Father calls Him My beloved Son, a dearly loved, only Son, and says He is well pleased, delighting in Him before His ministry has even begun.
basic.typology: Jesus' words echo Psalm 2, where God's King is the Son, and Isaiah 42, where the Servant receives the Spirit and the Father's delight. The Spirit descending recalls the dove over the waters at creation and after the flood, hinting at a new creation. Jesus enters the Jordan as the true Israel and the new Adam, beginning again where they fell.
basic.christ: The whole scene is a public declaration of who Jesus is: the beloved Son, anointed by the Spirit, approved by the Father. He is the King of Psalm 2 and the Servant of Isaiah 42 in one person. And by being baptized among sinners, He shows from the outset that He has come to stand in their place and bear their sin.
basic.god: God is revealed as three persons in one act: the Son is baptized, the Spirit descends, and the Father speaks. This is one of Scripture's plainest pictures of the Trinity. The Father's delight in the Son, spoken before any achievement, shows a love that is given, not earned, the very love into which the gospel brings us.
basic.crossReferences: Psalm 2:7 calls God's King His Son. Isaiah 42:1 is the Servant on whom God puts His Spirit and in whom He delights. Isaiah 61:1 speaks of the Spirit anointing God's messenger. And 2 Corinthians 5 says Christ, who knew no sin, was made sin for us.
basic.oneStory: At the Jordan the true Son begins the mission the first son, Israel, and the first man, Adam, failed. He steps into the sinners' place, is anointed by the Spirit, and hears the Father's love. Everything the Old Testament hoped for, a King, a Servant, a new beginning, converges here as Jesus sets out to fulfill all righteousness and save His people.

context.literary: The baptism follows directly from John's announcement of the mightier One, and Matthew stages it as the formal inauguration of Jesus' ministry. It pairs with the temptation that immediately follows: the Son declared here is at once tested there. The Father's words gather two great Old Testament streams, the royal Son and the suffering Servant.
context.historical: John baptized a baptism of repentance for the forgiveness of sins, which is precisely why his objection makes sense: the sinless Jesus needs no such cleansing. That Jesus insists anyway underscores that His baptism means something different, identification with sinners and obedience to the Father's plan, not personal repentance.
context.canonical: The scene is a cornerstone for the doctrine of the Trinity, showing Father, Son, and Spirit distinct yet united. The Father's declaration blends Psalm 2:7 and Isaiah 42:1, presenting Jesus as both the Davidic King and the Isaianic Servant. The anointing by the Spirit marks Him as the Christ, the Anointed One, empowered for His mission, Acts 10:38.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To mark the beginning of Jesus' ministry with a divine declaration of His identity as the beloved Son and Spirit-anointed Servant, and to show that His baptism, far from admitting sin, fulfills all righteousness by identifying Him with the people He came to save.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This is a unique, unrepeatable event, the anointing of the Messiah, not a template for our baptism, though it does dignify baptism and model glad obedience. What it prescribes to faith is belief in the triune God here revealed and in Jesus as the beloved Son.

debates.question: Why was the sinless Jesus baptized at all?

views.label: To identify with sinners and stand in their place
views.case: Though He had no sin to confess, Jesus steps into the sinners' baptism to unite Himself with the people He came to redeem, beginning the substitution that ends at the cross, 2 Corinthians 5:21.

views.label: To fulfill all righteousness by complete obedience
views.case: Jesus' own reason is that it fulfills all righteousness; He submits to every requirement of the Father's saving plan, modeling the total obedience that His whole life renders on our behalf.

debates.bearing: The two readings are complementary: by obediently taking the sinners' place, Jesus both fulfills righteousness and begins the representative work by which sinners are saved.

debates.question: What does the Father's declaration reveal about Jesus?

views.label: The royal Son of Psalm 2
views.case: You are My Son echoes the enthronement of God's King in Psalm 2:7, declaring Jesus the promised Davidic Messiah who will reign.

views.label: The beloved Servant of Isaiah 42
views.case: In whom I am well pleased echoes Isaiah 42:1, God's chosen Servant on whom He puts His Spirit, marking Jesus as the one who saves through humble, suffering obedience.

debates.bearing: Matthew intends both at once: Jesus is King and Servant, and His throne runs through His suffering, so that the glory declared here leads to the cross.

study.originalLanguages: Jesus explains His baptism as fulfilling all dikaiosyne, righteousness, doing everything the Father's plan of salvation requires and taking His place among the people He redeems. The pneuma, the Spirit of God, descends and rests on Him, the anointing that marks the promised Servant, Isaiah 42:1. Then the Father names Him agapetos, beloved, His dearly loved and only Son, and says He is eudokeo, well pleased, delighting in Him before He has preached a word or worked a sign. The Son's identity and the Father's love are settled before the mission begins.

typology.type: The Spirit descending like a dove
typology.fulfillment: Echoing the Spirit over the waters at creation and the dove after the flood, it signals new creation dawning in the Spirit-anointed Son, Genesis 1:2 and 8:8-12.

typology.type: The beloved Son at the water
typology.fulfillment: Jesus as the true Israel, God's son called out of Egypt and now beginning obediently where the nation failed, and the new Adam beginning the human story afresh.

typology.type: The King and Servant of Psalm 2 and Isaiah 42
typology.fulfillment: The Father's words unite the reigning Son and the suffering Servant in Jesus, the Christ anointed to save through obedience unto death.

study.christ: The baptism is a coronation and a commissioning. Heaven itself declares who Jesus is: the beloved Son, the Spirit-anointed Servant, the delight of His Father. He is the King of Psalm 2 and the Servant of Isaiah 42 in one person, and His throne will run straight through His suffering. Yet the most striking thing is where He stands, in the river among sinners, submitting to a baptism He does not need. From His first public act, Jesus takes the place of the guilty, beginning the great exchange in which the sinless One is numbered with transgressors so that transgressors may become the righteousness of God in Him.
study.god: In a single moment the one God is revealed as three persons: the Son rising from the water, the Spirit descending as a dove, the Father speaking from heaven. Here is the Trinity, distinct yet undivided, working together in the salvation of the world. And the Father's word is pure grace: He declares His delight in the Son before the ministry, the miracles, or the cross, showing a love that is given, not earned. This is the love in which the Son has always stood, and into which the gospel now draws all who are united to Him.

crossReferences.note: You are My Son; the enthronement of God's King, echoed in the Father's declaration.
My chosen Servant in whom I delight, on whom I put My Spirit, fulfilled at the baptism.
The Spirit of the Lord anoints God's messenger to proclaim good news, the anointing pictured here.
God made Him who knew no sin to be sin for us, the substitution His baptism among sinners foreshadows.

study.oneStory: At the Jordan the whole hope of Scripture converges. The true Son begins, in obedience, the mission that Adam and Israel failed; the promised King and Servant is anointed by the Spirit; and the Father's love rests openly on Him. By standing among sinners in the water, Jesus signals from the outset that He has come to take their place. The ministry that will end at the cross begins with heaven torn open and a Father's delight, and it moves from here toward the salvation of the world.

quiz.q: Why did John at first try to prevent Jesus from being baptized?

options.text: John knew Jesus was greater and sinless, and felt he himself needed to be baptized by Jesus.
options.note: Verse 14 records John's protest that the roles should be reversed.

options.text: John doubted whether Jesus was truly the Messiah.
options.note: John recognized Jesus' greatness; his hesitation was reverence, not doubt about His identity.

options.text: John's baptism was closed to people from Galilee.
options.note: There was no such restriction; John objected because Jesus was greater than he.

options.text: John refused to baptize anyone who had not confessed sin.
options.note: The point is that Jesus had no sin to confess, yet John's objection was about Jesus' superiority, not a rule.

quiz.q: What reason did Jesus give for being baptized?

options.text: It is fitting for us to fulfill all righteousness in this way.
options.note: Verse 15 gives Jesus' own explanation for submitting to baptism.

options.text: To set an example that all His followers must copy exactly.
options.note: His baptism does dignify obedience, but His stated reason was to fulfill all righteousness, not merely to set a pattern.

options.text: To repent of His own sins like everyone else.
options.note: Jesus was sinless; His baptism was not for personal repentance.

options.text: To be initiated into John's community of disciples.
options.note: He came to fulfill righteousness and begin His mission, not to join John's circle.

quiz.q: What happened as Jesus came up from the water?

options.text: Heaven opened, the Spirit descended like a dove, and the Father's voice spoke.
options.note: Verses 16-17 record all three at once, revealing Father, Son, and Spirit.

options.text: The Spirit descended and rested on Him in the sight of the crowd.
options.note: The Spirit did descend, but the fuller answer includes heaven opening and the Father's voice.

options.text: An angel appeared to announce His ministry.
options.note: It was the Father's own voice and the Spirit, not an angel.

options.text: Nothing visible occurred; Jesus simply departed.
options.note: The heavens opened and the Spirit descended with the Father's declaration.

quiz.q: What did the Father declare about Jesus?

options.text: This is My beloved Son, in whom I am well pleased.
options.note: Verse 17 gives the Father's declaration, uniting Psalm 2 and Isaiah 42.

options.text: This is My chosen Servant who will suffer for many.
options.note: The Servant theme is present by echo of Isaiah 42, but the actual words name Him beloved Son in whom the Father delights.

options.text: This is the prophet like Moses; listen to Him.
options.note: Those words come later at the Transfiguration's echo of Deuteronomy; here the Father declares Him beloved Son.

options.text: This is the King who will conquer Rome.
options.note: The Father declares sonship and delight, not military conquest.

quiz.q: What does the baptism reveal about God?

options.text: The one God as three persons: Father speaking, Son baptized, Spirit descending.
options.note: The scene is one of Scripture's clearest windows onto the Trinity at work together.

options.text: That God the Father dwells in heaven and sends messengers.
options.note: True but partial; the scene reveals the Father, Son, and Spirit together, not merely a distant Father.

options.text: That God rewards Jesus for the ministry He has completed.
options.note: The Father declares delight before the ministry begins; it is grace, not reward for work done.

options.text: That God is a single person who appears in different modes.
options.note: The three are present distinctly at once, not one person in changing modes.

questions: Jesus stepped into the sinners' place at His baptism though He had no sin. How does it move you that your Savior identified with you from the very start of His ministry?
The Father delighted in the Son before He had done any public work. How would resting in God's given love, rather than earning approval, change the way you live this week?
The whole Trinity was present at the Jordan. How does a clearer sight of Father, Son, and Spirit deepen your worship and prayer?
Jesus said His baptism fulfilled all righteousness, glad obedience to the Father's plan. Where is God calling you to obedience that may look unnecessary but honors Him?
The Spirit anointed Jesus for His mission. In what task or calling do you need to depend on the Spirit's power rather than your own strength?

translationNotes.note: Fulfill all righteousness renders a phrase meaning to carry out everything God requires; Jesus submits fully to the Father's saving plan.

The heavens were opened may also read the heavens were opened to Him, and He saw may also be rendered and he saw; the BSB footnotes note both, and John 1:32-33 records John's witness of the Spirit's descent.
The declaration blends Psalm 2:7, the royal Son, with Isaiah 42:1, the beloved Servant in whom God delights, presenting Jesus as both King and Servant.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Matthew 3:13-17. Verse text assembled by concatenating segment values in order; line-break and note markers were skipped. No em or en dashes appear. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
